title: Abolitionist Mass Meeting Notes
text: At the mass meeting in 1863, the crowd sang Negro spirituals like 'Go Down, Moses' to rally support for emancipation. Speakers observed that the familiar spirituals gave courage and a common language of hope.

title: Freedmen Schools Circular
text: Teachers reported using call-and-response spirituals to teach collective discipline and solidarity. One teacher wrote that spirituals made lessons "feel like freedom."

title: Southern Textile Workers Organizing Report
text: Union organizers adapted freedom songs from spirituals to sustain long meetings. The rhythm of spirituals kept spirits up during long votes.

title: Montgomery Bus Boycott Mass Meeting Transcript
text: Between speeches, the congregation sang 'We Shall Overcome' and other spirituals. An SCLC organizer noted that the old spirituals became "freedom songs" for the movement.

title: SNCC Nonviolence Workshop Notes
text: Workshop leaders used spirituals as training aids; freedom songs set expectations for group discipline. Participants said spirituals helped them "keep on keeping on."

title: Church Homecoming Program
text: The program listed a spirituals festival and a reading of testimonies from community organizers. While not a protest, the event linked spirituals to local organizing.

title: Sharecroppers' Strike Field Memo
text: Workers sang 'Which Side Are You On?' during pickets. Though not a spiritual, singing structured the march.
